Appearance
From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Squad list of 2021 Africa Cup of Nations national teams
2021 Africa Cup of Nations squads
← 2019
2023 →
International football competition

The 2021 Africa Cup of Nations was an international football tournament that was held in Cameroon from 9 January to 6 February 2022. The 24 national teams involved in the tournament were allowed to register a squad of up to 28 players, including a minimum of three goalkeepers, to allow teams to deal with any COVID-19 cases. Only players in these squads were eligible to take part in the tournament.[1]

The position and date of birth listed for each player is per the official squad list published by CAF. The age listed for each player is on 9 January 2022, the first day of the tournament. The numbers of caps and goals listed for each player do not include any matches played after the start of tournament. The club listed is the club for which the player last played a competitive match prior to the tournament. The nationality for each club reflects the national association (not the league) to which the club is affiliated. A flag is included for coaches that are of a different nationality than their own national team.

Group A

[edit]

Burkina Faso

[edit]

Coach: Kamou Malo

A 29-man provisional squad was announced on 24 December 2021.[2] The final 28-man squad was named on 4 January 2022.[3][4]

Cape Verde

[edit]

Coach: Bubista

A 26-man final squad was announced on 23 December 2021.[7] On 3 January 2022, Delmiro and Elber Evora were added to the squad to reach the maximum of 28 players.[8] On 7 January 2022, Djaniny withdrew due to injury and was replaced by Vagner Gonçalves.[9]

Group B

[edit]

Guinea

Coach: Kaba Diawara

Malawi

[edit]

Coach: Mario Marinică

A 43-man provisional squad, including 13 standby players, was announced on 22 December 2021.[13] A 23-man final squad was announced on 1 January 2022.[14] Additionally to the squad named for the tournament, 5 players (Stain Davie, Paul Ndhlovu, Gerald Phiri Jr., Brighton Munthali, and Daniel Austin Chimbalanga) were named as reserves and will be available in case of eventualities.[15]

[edit]

Coach: Vahid Halilhodžić

A 25-man final squad was announced on 23 December 2021.[26] On 28 December 2021, Badr Benoun, Mohamed Chibi, and Soufiane Rahimi were added to the squad to reach the maximum of 28 players.[27] On 30 December 2021, Anas Zniti withdrew due to injury and was replaced by Ahmed Reda Tagnaouti.[28] On 10 January 2022, Badr Benoun withdrew due to injury and was replaced by Achraf Bencharki.[29]

Group D

[edit]

Egypt

[edit]

Coach: Carlos Queiroz

A 40-man provisional squad was announced on 19 December 2021.[30] A 25-man final squad was announced on 29 December 2021.[31] On the next day, Ibrahim Adel, Marwan Hamdy and Mohamed Hamdy were added to the squad to reach the maximum of 28 players.[32] On 7 January 2022, Mohamed Hamdy withdrew from the squad due to a knee injury and was replaced by Marwan Dawoud.[33]

Nigeria

[edit]

Interim coach: Augustine Eguavoen

A 28-man final squad was announced on 25 December 2021.[35] On 31 December 2021, Emmanuel Dennis, Victor Osimhen, Leon Balogun, and Shehu Abdullahi withdrew from the squad and were replaced by Semi Ajayi, Tyronne Ebuehi, Peter Olayinka, and Henry Onyekuru.[36] On 6 January 2022, Odion Ighalo withdrawn but remain in the squad, thus reducing the squad to 27 players.[37] Newly appointed coach José Peseiro joined the team as an observer, while interim coach Eguavoen led the team.[38]

Ivory Coast

[edit]

Coach: Patrice Beaumelle

A 28-man final squad was announced on 23 December 2021.[45] On 31 December 2021, Sylvain Gbohouo was temporarily banned by FIFA, due to being found guilty of taking the prohibited substance trimetazidine and could not participate.[46] A few days later, N'Drin Ulrich Edan was called up to replace him.[47]

Tunisia

[edit]

Coach: Mondher Kebaier

A 28-man final squad was announced on 30 December 2021.[56] On 4 January 2022, it was announced that Youssef Msakni and Seifeddine Jaziri tested positive for COVID-19, but the Tunisian Football Federation were not allowed to replace them ahead of the tournament.[57] On 5 January 2022, Firas Ben Larbi withdrew due to injury and was replaced by Issam Jebali.[58]

  2. ^ "29 players in Burkina Faso provisional TotalEnergies AFCON squad". Confederation of African Football. 24 December 2021. Archived from the original on 8 January 2022. Retrieved 27 December 2021.
  3. ^ Ali, Ahmad Gamal (4 January 2022). "Egyptian Premier League duo make Burkina Faso final squad for AFCON". KingFut. Archived from the original on 4 January 2022. Retrieved 9 January 2022.
  4. ^ "TotalEnergies AFCON 2021 - Burkina Faso squad" (PDF). Confederation of African Football. 5 January 2022. Archived (PDF) from the original on 6 January 2022. Retrieved 9 January 2022.
  5. ^ Raubil, Gariba (10 December 2021). "AFCON 2021 : Host nation Cameroon names 40-man provisional squad". Sports World Ghana. Archived from the original on 27 December 2021. Retrieved 27 December 2021.
  6. ^ "TotalEnergies AFCON 2021: Cameroon names final squad". Confederation of African Football. 22 December 2021. Archived from the original on 18 January 2022. Retrieved 27 December 2021.
  7. ^ "Cape Verde include veteran quartet for AFCON". ESPN. 23 December 2021. Archived from the original on 26 December 2021. Retrieved 26 December 2021.
  8. ^ "Elber Évora e Delmiro Nascimento reforçam selecção nacional" [Elber Évora and Delmiro Nascimento reinforce national team]. Expresso das Ilhas (in Portuguese). 3 January 2022. Archived from the original on 10 January 2022. Retrieved 10 January 2022.
  9. ^ "Vagner Dias chamado à seleção de Cabo Verde para render Djaniny" [Vagner Dias called to the Cape Verde national team to replace Djaniny]. O Jogo (in Portuguese). 7 January 2022. Archived from the original on 10 January 2022. Retrieved 10 January 2022.
  10. ^ "Ethiopia include two foreign-based players in AFCON squad". ESPN. 24 December 2021. Archived from the original on 26 December 2021. Retrieved 26 December 2021.

  12. ^ "Soccer-Guinea suffer two withdraws from Cup of Nations squad". Reuters. 26 December 2021. Archived from the original on 26 December 2021. Retrieved 26 December 2021.
  13. ^ Oludare, Shina (22 December 2021). "Afcon 2021: Orlando Pirates' Mhango headlines Malawi's provisional squad". Goal. Archived from the original on 27 December 2021. Retrieved 27 December 2021.
  14. ^ "Malawi defeat Comoros in a friendly, unveil final TotalEnergies AFCON squad". Confederation of African Football. 1 January 2022. Archived from the original on 13 October 2022. Retrieved 4 January 2022.
  15. ^ "Malawi name final squad for Cameroon". Council of Southern Africa Football Associations. 31 December 2021. Archived from the original on 10 January 2022. Retrieved 10 January 2022.
  16. ^ "Cisse unveils Senegal squad, hopes for elusive TotalEnergies AFCON title". Confederation of African Football. 25 December 2021. Archived from the original on 9 January 2022. Retrieved 29 December 2021.
  17. ^ "Alioune Badara Faty du Casa Sports représente la Ligue 1 à la CAN" [Alioune Badara Faty from Casa Sports represents Ligue 1 at CAN]. Galsenfoot (in French). 5 January 2022. Archived from the original on 2022-01-10. Retrieved 10 January 2022.
  18. ^ "Musona leads Zimbabwe Warriors' provisional squad". Confederation of African Football. 21 December 2021. Archived from the original on 26 December 2021. Retrieved 26 December 2021.
  19. ^ "Afcon 2021: A Zimbabwe squad is named despite threat of a Fifa ban". BBC Sport. 29 December 2021. Archived from the original on 4 January 2022. Retrieved 4 January 2022.
  20. ^ "Three players placed on standby in Warriors squad for Afcon 2021". Soccer24. 8 January 2022. Archived from the original on 9 January 2022. Retrieved 9 January 2022.
  21. ^ "Comoros debut TotalEnergies AFCON squad unveiled". Confederation of African Football. 23 December 2021. Archived from the original on 27 December 2021. Retrieved 27 December 2021.
  22. ^ Ismail, Ali (23 December 2021). "Comoros announce squad for first ever Africa Cup of Nations". KingFut. Archived from the original on 24 December 2021. Retrieved 4 January 2022.
  23. ^ Oludare, Shina (18 December 2021). "Afcon 2021: Arsenal's Aubameyang, Brighton's Ella headline Gabon provisional squad". Goal. Archived from the original on 29 December 2021. Retrieved 29 December 2021.
  24. ^ "30 players in Rajevac preliminary Ghana squad for TotalEnergies AFCON". Confederation of African Football. 21 December 2021. Archived from the original on 29 December 2021. Retrieved 29 December 2021.
  25. ^ "Milovan Rajevac names squad for Total Energies 2021 AFCON". Ghana Football Association. 3 January 2022.
  26. ^ "Hakim Ziyech: Chelsea forward not included in Morocco's squad for Africa Cup of Nations". Eurosport. 23 December 2021. Archived from the original on 23 December 2021. Retrieved 28 December 2021.
  27. ^ "Afcon 2021: Morocco add three to squad for Cameroon". BBC Sport. 28 December 2021. Archived from the original on 29 December 2021. Retrieved 28 December 2021.
  28. ^ "إستدعاء الحارس أحمد رضى التكناوتي لمعسكر أسود الأطلس" [The goalkeeper, Ahmed Reda Tagnaouti, has been summoned to the Atlas Lions camp] (in Arabic). Royal Moroccan Football Federation. 30 December 2021. Archived from the original on 30 December 2021. Retrieved 2 January 2022.
  29. ^ "الناخب الوطني يستدعي أشرف بنشرقي لتعويض بدر بانون" [The national coach calls on Achraf Bencharki to replace Badr Benoun] (in Arabic). Royal Moroccan Football Federation. 10 January 2022. Archived from the original on 11 January 2022. Retrieved 11 January 2022.
  30. ^ "Hamed left-out, Trezeguet returns in Queiroz's preliminary AFCON squad". KingFut. 19 December 2021. Archived from the original on 29 December 2021. Retrieved 29 December 2021.
  31. ^ "Afcon 2021: Aston Villa's Trezeguet returns to join Salah and Elneny in Egypt squad". BBC Sport. 29 December 2021. Archived from the original on 31 December 2021. Retrieved 29 December 2021.
  32. ^ "Egypt coach Carlos Queiroz calls up three new players for AFCON". KingFut. 31 December 2021. Archived from the original on 31 January 2022. Retrieved 31 December 2021.
  33. ^ "إنبي يعلن استدعاء مروان داوود لـ منتخب مصر" [ENPPI announce national team call-up for Marwan Dawoud]. FilGoal (in Arabic). 7 January 2022. Archived from the original on 7 January 2022. Retrieved 7 January 2022.
  34. ^ "Afcon 2021: Guinea-Bissau include Moreto Cassama in squad". BBC Sport. 30 December 2021. Archived from the original on 24 January 2022. Retrieved 30 December 2021.

  40. ^ "CAN 2022. La liste complète du Soudan, avec deux joueurs évoluant en Europe" [CAN 2022: The full squad of Sudan, with two players playing in Europe]. Ouest-France (in French). 5 January 2022. Archived from the original on 5 January 2022. Retrieved 5 January 2022.

  43. ^ "Equatorial Guinea "Nzalang" announce TotalEnergies AFCON squad". Confederation of African Football. 27 December 2021. Archived from the original on 24 January 2022. Retrieved 27 December 2021.

  47. ^ "CAN 2022. Côte d'Ivoire : suspendu pour dopage, le gardien ivoirien privé de compétition" [CAN 2022. Ivory Coast: suspended for doping, the Ivorian goalkeeper deprived of competition]. Ouest-France (in French). 2 January 2022. Archived from the original on 13 October 2022. Retrieved 17 January 2022. Dans le doute et dans l'attente d'une décision définitive, Patrice Beaumelle a décidé d'appeler Edan Ulrich N'Drin, un cinquième gardien, en renfort. [In doubt and awaiting a final decision, Patrice Beaumelle decided to call Edan Ulrich N'Drin, a fifth goalkeeper, as reinforcements.]

Notes
The 2021 and 2023 tournaments were actually held in 2022 and 2024 respectively.
There were no 1957 and 1959 qualifications as places were given by invitation only.
The "finals" articles for 1959 and 1976 are about the decisive matches of final group stages.
